description: Ontology for VP Finance-level LTV modeling: dynamic LTV with cohort decay, churn prediction signals, expansion revenue models (NRR), and the finance-architecture intersection.

concepts.name: Dynamic Lifetime Value
concepts.description: Unlike static LTV (ARPU × Lifetime × Margin), dynamic LTV accounts for variable churn rates per period, expansion revenue after month 6-12, and margin evolution. Cohort analysis is mandatory.

concepts.name: Static Lifetime Value (Dangerous Simplification)
concepts.description: Assumes constant churn, constant revenue, zero expansion. Acceptable only for MVP-stage estimates. Dangerous for fundraising or strategic decisions.

concepts.name: Net Revenue Retention
concepts.description: The single most important SaaS metric. NRR > 100% means you grow without acquiring new customers. Elite SaaS: 115-130%. Mediocre: 90-100%. Dying: < 90%.

concepts.name: Churn Prediction Model
concepts.description: Mature companies don't say 'we have 5% churn'. They say 'this customer has 72% probability of churn in 90 days'. This changes how you allocate retention resources.

concepts.name: Expansion Revenue
concepts.description: Revenue increase from existing customers through upsells, cross-sells, and usage growth. The true multiplier — you can have high churn and still grow if expansion compensates (Negative Net Revenue Churn).

models.seat_based: More users → more revenue. Natural in B2B. Risk: if no internal adoption, massive churn.
models.usage_based: Pay per consumption (API calls, storage). Organic expansion but unpredictable revenue.
models.tiered_upgrade: Basic → Pro → Enterprise. Clear upsell path but needs real feature gap.

concepts.name: Cohort Analysis
concepts.description: Mandatory investor-level analysis. Track retention, ARPU evolution, and expansion by monthly cohort. Weak startup: retention curve falls fast, never stabilizes. Strong startup: small initial drop, then plateau. The plateau defines real LTV.

concepts.name: Churn-Architecture Intersection
concepts.description: Churn in first 30 days = onboarding/UX problem (V65). Churn after 6 months = lack of expansion value. Expansion depends on usage intensity = architecture must support cheap scalability. Low margin from infrastructure = LTV collapses. Finance and architecture are inseparable.
